Glossary About
Renaissance & Late Scholasticism

Teresa of Ávila

1515 – 1582

Interior prayer, self-knowledge, and the soul’s ascent through inward castles.

Teresa of Ávila does not write a “philosophy of mind” in the modern academic sense. She writes as a Carmelite reformer, contemplative, director of souls, and vernacular theologian of prayer. Yet her writings contain a remarkably coherent account of the human person, mental powers, attention, imagination, affect, self-knowledge, pathological disturbance, embodied experience, supernatural cognition, discernment, and the relation of human consciousness to divine, angelic, and demonic intelligences. The result is not a detachable psychology with a religious overlay. For Teresa, the theological structure is the philosophy: the mind is what it is because the soul is created for, inhabited by, addressed by, and finally transformed by God.

The most compact thesis is this: Teresa’s “mind” is an internally differentiated, embodied, relational soul whose surface operations include understanding, will, memory, imagination, thought, senses, and passions, but whose deepest center is not reducible to these operations. The mature soul is not the self-transparent Cartesian thinker, but an inhabited interior castle whose center is stabilized by God while its peripheral faculties may still be noisy, distracted, tired, or embattled. Teresa’s account is therefore a layered, participatory, theistic, affective, and contemplative philosophy of mind.

Teresa’s importance is not merely devotional. Paul VI’s 1970 proclamation of Teresa as Doctor of the Church explicitly emphasizes her “eminence of doctrine,” her teaching on prayer, and the psychologically delicate complexity of mystical states in which Teresa is sometimes not the agent but the recipient of an extraordinary divine action. That is directly relevant to any philosophical reconstruction of her account of mind, because passivity, receptivity, infused cognition, and love are central to her account of higher mental life.

1. Methodological cautions

The first caution is lexical. Teresa’s alma is not simply “mind,” “psyche,” “self,” “consciousness,” or “personality.” It includes what modern philosophy might divide among mind, person, subject, agency, moral character, affective orientation, spiritual capacity, and embodied life. Her entendimiento is often “understanding” or “intellect,” but it does not always map neatly onto modern reason. Her pensamiento and imaginación overlap with discursive thought, mental image, involuntary ideation, and attention. Her voluntad is not mere volition in the thin sense of choosing; it is the affective-appetitive power by which the soul loves, consents, clings, desires, and is ordered toward God.

The second caution is genre. Teresa’s central texts—especially Libro de la Vida, Camino de Perfección, Las Moradas or Interior Castle, and the Relaciones—are practical, autobiographical, ascetical, mystical, and doctrinal at once. She frequently says “it seems to me,” defers to learned theologians, and treats experiential accuracy as inseparable from humility and ecclesial testing. In one passage on union, she even says that learned theologians are given by God as lights for the Church and that she is ready to accept correction if she is wrong.

The third caution is that Teresa’s theology cannot be stripped away without destroying the theory. God, Christ, the Trinity, angels, demons, grace, and prayer are not optional symbolic ornaments. They function as agents, causes, sources of knowledge, norms of discernment, and poles of intentionality in her mental ontology. A secularized “Teresa of attention” or “Teresa of introspection” is possible as an approximation, but it is not Teresa’s full account.

2. The soul as interior architecture

Teresa’s most famous image is the soul as a castle “of diamond or very clear crystal,” containing many rooms or dwelling places, with God at the center. She also says the soul of the just person is a paradise in which God takes delight. This is not a merely decorative metaphor. It gives Teresa an ontology of interiority: the soul has depth, structure, zones of access, thresholds, dangers, movement, and a center.

The castle image also resists a flat model of consciousness. Teresa’s soul is not identical with whatever thoughts are currently passing through awareness. It is possible to be “inside” or “outside” oneself, to live at the circumference or move toward the center, to confuse peripheral noise with the state of the whole soul, and to be nearer to God than one’s surface thought-stream would suggest. This is why Teresa can say that thought may be in the outskirts of the castle while the soul itself is near God.

Her anthropology is therefore topological. The mind is not simply a series of mental events. It is an ordered interior world. The soul can be more or less recollected, more or less dispersed, more or less at peace in its own “house.” When Teresa asks whether there can be a greater evil than not finding ourselves in our own house, she is describing a condition of inner alienation: the faculties with which we must live—understanding, will, memory, imagination—can become like friends and relatives who make war within us.

This is a major philosophical point. Teresa’s theory of mind is not primarily about isolated mental representations. It is about inhabitation. A sound mind is one in which the person can dwell rightly within herself before God. A disordered mind is one in which the person is dispersed, exiled from her own center, or tyrannized by unruly faculties.

3. The soul is more than its faculties

One of Teresa’s most philosophically important claims appears in the seventh mansions: “the soul is different from the faculties,” or at least “it seems” to her that it is not all one thing. She also distinguishes, very delicately, between soul and spirit, while refusing to overdefine the distinction. Her language is cautious, but the conclusion is strong: the subject is not reducible to its operations.

This matters enormously. Teresa does not identify the person with the stream of thought, with discursive understanding, with memory, with imagination, or even with the will in isolation. These are potencias, powers or faculties. They are essential to ordinary mental life, but they are not the deepest reality of the soul.

A reconstructed Teresian philosophy of mind should therefore distinguish at least four levels.

First, there are the external senses and bodily conditions: sight, hearing, speech, fatigue, illness, headaches, breath, posture, somatic delight, weakness, tears, and ecstasy.

Second, there are the mobile mental powers: understanding, will, memory, imagination, and thought.

Third, there is the soul as the personal-spiritual subject that has these faculties but is not identical with them.

Fourth, there is the center or spirit, the deepest interior point where God communicates himself most intimately, especially in the seventh mansions. Teresa admits that this “center” or “spirit” is difficult even to express or believe, but she insists that one can experience peace there while other powers and passions remain in conflict.

This is not a modern “modular mind” theory, but it is a sophisticated account of stratified consciousness. Teresa sees the person as internally plural without being metaphysically fragmented. The soul is “all together,” yet there can be a delicate difference between soul and spirit, and between the soul and its powers.

4. Faculty psychology: understanding, will, memory, imagination, thought

Teresa inherits and adapts a traditional faculty psychology, but she modifies it through experience. The usual triad is understanding, will, and memory. But Teresa repeatedly adds, isolates, and worries over pensamiento and imaginación. Her most decisive experiential discovery is that thought or imagination is not the same as understanding. She says she was relieved when a learned theologian confirmed this distinction, because she had suffered from the “uproar” of thought and had assumed that the whole understanding was disordered when the imagination was merely restless.

This is one of her most valuable contributions to philosophy of mind: she separates involuntary mental motion from rational or spiritual attention. Wandering thoughts do not necessarily mean that the soul has failed, nor that the understanding is not directed toward God, nor that prayer is ruined. Teresa observes that the faculties can be recollected in God while thought remains noisy. She even compares thought’s motion to the rapid movement of the heavens: it cannot simply be stopped by command.

The understanding, in Teresa, is the discursive and interpretive faculty. It reasons, considers, meditates, compares, searches for causes, reads, applies Scripture, and tries to make sense. In early prayer, this is necessary: the soul works “with the understanding,” reflecting on Christ, sin, death, gratitude, and divine mercy. Teresa does not despise this. She says meditation and discursive understanding are appropriate in earlier mansions, and she warns against abandoning the faculties before God has elevated them.

But the understanding also has limits. It can become a “grinder,” making too much noise in prayer. In the prayer of quiet, Teresa advises that the will should not chase after the understanding when it is restless; the will should remain in its quiet enjoyment of God.

The will is the affective center of love, consent, and surrender. It can be “captive” to God in prayer of quiet while the other faculties remain active. Teresa’s will is not merely the executive faculty of choice. It is the faculty of love, adherence, and conformity. The soul’s highest perfection consists in conformity of the will to God’s will, not in having extraordinary experiences.

Memory is both necessary and troublesome. It preserves the history of salvation, one’s sins, Christ’s humanity, gratitude, and lessons learned. But it can also importune the soul, revive useless images, and disturb prayer. Teresa often links memory with imagination; in some states of union, memory remains free and becomes a source of harassment. In deeper suspension, even memory loses its wings, as Teresa puts it through the image of the importunate little moth whose wings are burned.

Imagination and thought are especially unstable. Teresa does not simply demonize them. Imagination helps meditation, especially representation of Christ and biblical scenes. But it can also produce “little lizards” or minor intrusive thoughts that slip into the earlier mansions. In true union, however, Teresa says imagination, memory, and understanding cannot impede the good being given, because God is joined to the essence of the soul.

Spanish scholarship has developed this point with care. Guillermo Serés, for example, emphasizes that imagination and memory in Teresa are not merely obstacles; they are also contiguous with and preparatory for intellectual meditation and prayer. He situates Teresa within older theories linking imagination, memory, passions, bodily alteration, and the classical tripartite model of cerebral powers.

A useful Teresian taxonomy is therefore this:

Teresian term Function in mental life Typical disorder Higher transformation Alma Whole personal-spiritual subject Living outside itself; self-ignorance Becomes recollected, centered, God-inhabited Espíritu / centro Deepest interior point Hard to describe; hidden from ordinary introspection Stable peace and union in the seventh mansions Entendimiento Discursive understanding, reflection, interpretation Noisy analysis, fatigue, over-control Infused light, non-discursive understanding Voluntad Love, consent, desire, affective adherence Self-will, self-love, divided desire Captivity to God, conformity to divine will Memoria Retention, representation, recollection Importune recall, distraction, scruple Gratitude, Christic memory, eventual quieting Imaginación / pensamiento Image-flow, thought-stream, representational activity Restlessness, intrusive mental motion Useful meditation; later suspension or harmless periphery Senses and passions Embodied interface with world Distraction, agitation, fear, melancholy Recollection, ordered affect, overflow of grace into body

5. Attention and recollection

Teresa’s account of attention is one of the richest parts of her thought. Prayer is not, for her, mere verbal recitation, nor blank silence, nor discursive analysis alone. In Camino de Perfección, she defines mental prayer as knowing and attending to the fact that one is speaking with God: one must know with whom one speaks and who one is who dares to speak with such a Lord. Vocal and mental prayer are not mutually exclusive; vocal prayer becomes mental when understanding and heart are present in the words.

Recollection is not the annihilation of mental life. It is the gathering of the powers into the interior. Teresa says recollection gathers the soul’s powers and brings the soul within itself with God. It withdraws the senses from external distraction, not because the body is evil, but because attention must be reoriented toward the indwelling God. Her image of bees returning to the hive is a psychologically precise account of attention being gathered into a stable interior object.

French Carmelite commentary rightly stresses that Teresa’s meditation is not a technique whose goal is to stop all mental action. It applies thought, memory, imagination, and intelligence in a relational movement of the heart; the point is not mental blankness but personal relation with God.

This is crucial for comparing Teresa with modern contemplative theories. She is not teaching a generic quieting of cognition. She is teaching trained, relational attention: the mind recollects itself because it is addressing, and being addressed by, a personal God. The object of attention is not an abstract void but a living interlocutor.

6. Prayer as a developmental psychology of mind

Teresa’s degrees of prayer and mansions form a developmental account of mental transformation.

In the early stages, the person is scattered. The senses, passions, and thoughts pull outward. The soul has not yet learned to dwell in its own house. Discursive meditation is needed. One thinks about Christ, one’s sins, God’s mercy, death, judgment, gratitude, and love. This is not a lower thing to be despised; it is ordinary, necessary mental labor.

In the prayer of quiet, God begins to act in a more directly supernatural way. The soul begins to be recollected; the faculties are not lost or asleep, but the will is occupied and held by God. Teresa says the will is “captive” while the other faculties may help or hinder.

This is a psychologically subtle state: one faculty can be deeply at rest while others remain mobile. Teresa’s advice is not to force all mental functions into uniform stillness. The will should not abandon its quiet in order to chase the understanding or memory. This produces a model of partial integration: the mind can be asymmetrically transformed.

In more advanced union, God may “seize” the will and even the understanding, while memory and imagination remain free and troublesome. Teresa compares this to a soul partly joined to God and partly harassed by its own residual mental motion.

In the deeper suspension of all powers, the understanding does not operate discursively, memory does not retain its ordinary contents, imagination is absent, and the will loves without knowing how it loves. Teresa’s famous formulation is that the soul “does not understand by understanding,” or “understands by not understanding,” because the content exceeds ordinary comprehension.

In the seventh mansions, spiritual marriage is not merely a temporary altered state. It is a stabilized transformation of the soul’s center. Teresa says the secret union occurs in the very interior center of the soul, where God himself dwells, and that this does not enter by the ordinary “door” of senses and faculties.

Here Teresa gives a metaphysics of contemplative consciousness: the highest transformation is not the most intense psychological episode, but the deepest ontological stabilization of the soul in God. Ecstasy, rapture, delight, and visions may occur along the way, but the final mark is peace, conformity of will, service, humility, love, and freedom from self-absorption.

7. Consciousness is layered, not transparent

Teresa is not a Cartesian introspectionist. She does not think the self is immediately transparent to itself. In fact, much suffering in prayer comes from not understanding oneself. She says many people suffer because they do not realize there is an interior world within; they confuse wandering thought with the whole soul, blame themselves for what is not blameworthy, and may even fall into melancholy or abandon prayer because they lack a proper map of mental life.

This is one of her strongest philosophical contributions. Teresa distinguishes the subject from the contents of consciousness. A stray thought is not necessarily an act of the will. A restless imagination is not identical with a corrupted understanding. A distracted surface does not prove that the soul’s deeper orientation is false. The person may be praying, loving, and consenting at one level while involuntary thought-noise continues at another.

This has clear implications for guilt, agency, and mental health. Teresa’s model reduces false culpability. She repeatedly counsels patience with intrusive thought. The remedy is often not violent suppression but non-identification: do not make much of it, do not chase it, do not let it define the state of the soul. This is not indifference to sin; it is a careful discrimination between involuntary mental motion and voluntary consent.

Teresa’s center/periphery distinction reaches its height in the seventh mansions, where she says the soul can remain at peace while the senses, passions, and outer mansions still suffer wars and labors.

A contemporary philosopher might describe this as a distinction between phenomenal turbulence and deep dispositional orientation. Teresa would describe it more strongly: the true center of the soul can be held by God even while the lower or peripheral powers remain subject to conflict.

8. The will as affective cognition

Modern readers often divide cognition and emotion too sharply. Teresa does not. In her highest states, the will may know by loving, while the understanding cannot analyze what is happening. In the prayer of quiet, the will rests in God while the understanding may be noisy or unable to comprehend. In perfect contemplation, the soul enjoys without knowing how it enjoys, loves without knowing how it loves, and recognizes that the gift is beyond what the understanding could desire or merit.

This suggests an affective epistemology. Love is not merely a feeling added to cognition; love can be a mode of contact with reality. The will can be more rightly ordered toward truth than the discursive understanding is able to articulate. Teresa is not anti-intellectual. She values learning, confessors, Scripture, and theological discernment. But she denies that discursive reason is the highest or only mode of knowing.

Her famous claim that “the thing is not to think much, but to love much” must not be misread as irrationalism. In context, she is distinguishing spiritual progress from sheer quantity of discursive mental activity. The decisive issue is what awakens love of God, conformity to God’s will, and concrete virtue.

This gives Teresa a hierarchy of cognition:

Ordinary discursive cognition is necessary but limited.

Affective cognition through love can be more interiorly transformative.

Infused cognition is given by God, not manufactured.

The truth of higher cognition is tested by effects: humility, peace, love, obedience, perseverance, and service.

9. Infused knowledge and non-discursive understanding

Teresa repeatedly describes states in which the understanding ceases to work in its ordinary discursive way, yet the soul is not ignorant. Rather, God gives “a light in knowledge” beyond what human effort can attain. She explicitly warns against trying to enchant or paralyze the faculties by human technique; God gave the powers to do their work until God places them in a greater office.

This is a precise distinction between artificial passivity and infused passivity. Teresa opposes self-induced mental blankness. She permits the understanding to cease only when God occupies it by another mode. In other words, the cessation of discursive cognition is not itself the goal; the goal is receptive participation in a higher, divinely given cognition.

Her account of intellectual vision in the seventh mansions is especially important. She says the Trinity is shown to the soul by an intellectual vision, not by bodily eyes and not by an imaginative vision. The soul understands with great truth that the three persons are one substance, one power, one knowledge, and one God.

This is not sensory perception, not imagination, and not inference. It is presented as a direct, non-imagistic, intellectual disclosure. Whether one accepts the theological claim or not, as a philosophy of mind Teresa is describing a category of conscious content that is neither sensory nor imagistic nor discursive, but still cognitive and truth-bearing.

Recent scholarship continues to take Teresa’s classification of supernatural communications seriously. Chloe Lynch’s 2025 article, for example, treats Teresa’s classification of supernatural communications and her discernment criteria for imaginative and intellectual visions and locutions as a conceptual framework rather than as mere devotional anecdote.

10. Visions, locutions, and discernment as epistemology

Teresa is deeply concerned with the possibility of error. Her writings on locutions and visions are not naïve. She distinguishes voices and visions that may come from God, from the devil, from imagination, or from melancholy. She tests them by their mode of arrival, their effects, their conformity with Scripture, their aftermath in the soul, and the judgment of learned spiritual directors.

Her epistemology is therefore neither pure internalism nor crude externalism. The immediate experience matters, but it is not sufficient. Content matters, but content is not sufficient either, since false communications can appear pious. Effects matter: divine communications leave peace, humility, light, and strengthened virtue, whereas deceptive ones produce disturbance, vanity, agitation, or disordered attachment. Community matters: Teresa repeatedly advises consultation with learned and spiritual confessors.

Scholars such as Jill Robson have noted that Teresa’s treatment of visions is especially concerned with phenomenological characteristics, consequences, and judgment, not only with the content of the vision itself. Sandra Schneiders similarly emphasizes that Teresa differentiates intellectual visions, which communicate interior knowledge and certitude, from imaginative visions, which involve image-like or quasi-visual features, while maintaining the centrality of Christ’s humanity against overly disembodied spiritualization.

This gives Teresa a mature epistemology of extraordinary mental events. She does not say, “I experienced it, therefore it is true.” Nor does she say, “It is extraordinary, therefore it is false.” She constructs a discernment procedure: classify the mental event, examine its source, test its effects, submit it to theological judgment, and attend to whether it produces charity and conformity to God.

11. The mind-body relation

Teresa is not a Cartesian dualist in the simple sense. She certainly distinguishes soul and body, and in some states she says the soul seems to be separated from bodily operations. But her actual descriptions are deeply embodied. Prayer affects breath, speech, hands, strength, tears, bodily delight, illness, and sensory awareness. In her Relación 5, she says that in union the understanding is stunned, the will loves more than it understands, memory is almost gone, thought is absent, senses are not awake, and bodily signs can include cold hands and altered breathing.

In Libro de la Vida, Teresa says that in more intense prayer the exterior forces are lost and the forces of the soul increase; she also says the body participates in the delight of the soul.

This is not body-hatred. The body is a participant in spiritual life, even when it is also a source of distraction or limitation. Grace can overflow into the body; bodily weakness can also become a condition through which the soul learns its limits. Teresa’s mind-body account is therefore asymmetrical but interactive: the soul is not reducible to the body, but mental and spiritual states are embodied, expressed, hindered, and sometimes intensified through bodily conditions.

Her treatment of melancholy is also important. Teresa recognizes that some disturbances arise from bodily or temperamental conditions rather than sin or demonic deception. In Camino, she refers to bad humors, melancholy, and weakness of the head as conditions in which a person may be unable to settle the understanding despite effort; in such cases she advises not harsh self-blame but gentler care and other works of virtue.

This makes Teresa’s psychology pastorally sophisticated. She has categories for sin, weakness, illness, imagination, demonic deception, and divine action. She does not collapse them into one another.

12. Self-knowledge

Self-knowledge is indispensable for Teresa, but it is not mere introspection. To know oneself is to know oneself before God: one’s misery, dignity, dependence, capacity, and vocation. The soul must know both its poverty and its grandeur. It is humble not because it denies grace, but because it receives grace as gift.

French Carmelite interpretation captures this well: Teresian self-knowledge is not introspection in the modern individualist sense, but a faith-informed recognition of the soul’s greatness because it is inhabited by divine majesty.

In the castle image, self-knowledge and God-knowledge are inseparable. One cannot enter the castle without knowing oneself, yet the whole reason to enter is that God dwells there. The self is not autonomous interiority; it is relational interiority. One discovers oneself by discovering the Guest within.

This differs sharply from a purely Cartesian starting point. Teresa does not begin from “I think, therefore I am.” A Teresian starting point would be closer to: “I am an interior creature, often opaque to myself, whose deepest truth is disclosed in relation to the God who dwells in the center of the soul.” Modern scholars have noticed Teresa’s philosophical relevance here, though the literature remains comparatively limited; a 2025 philosophical article by Emily Agoff notes that Teresa has received little philosophical attention and contrasts Teresa’s interest in passive, supernatural cognitive states with Descartes’s methodological distancing from such reliance.

13. The mind of God in Teresa’s philosophy of mind

Teresa’s account of God is not merely a doctrine external to psychology. God is the supreme knowing, willing, loving, communicating subject, and the human mind is intelligible only in relation to this divine mind.

Several claims follow.

First, God knows and acts in the soul more deeply than the soul knows or acts upon itself. In spiritual marriage, God communicates in the center of the soul without needing the ordinary “door” of senses and faculties.

Second, divine speech is performative. Teresa says that the Lord’s words are “works” in us: they do what they say. This is a profound theory of divine language. Human words may merely signify; divine words effect transformation.

Third, God’s knowledge gives knowledge. God can open the “window of the understanding” when he wills, so that the soul knows the divine presence even when it cannot keep that clarity continuously at will.

Fourth, the divine mind is Trinitarian. Teresa’s intellectual vision of the Trinity is not a side-topic. It becomes a claim about the highest form of cognition available to the human soul: non-imaginative, non-bodily, intellectual participation in the truth of the tri-personal God. She describes the soul as understanding that the three persons are one substance, one power, one knowledge, and one God.

Fifth, God’s will is the norm of mental and moral health. The perfection of the soul is not maximal inner experience but conformity of the will to God’s will. Teresa says this is the whole good of the spiritual path.

For a Teresian philosophy of mind, then, God is not merely the object of belief. God is the ground, interlocutor, teacher, healer, illuminator, and final attractor of the mind. The human mind becomes fully itself not by self-enclosure but by participation in the divine life.

14. Christ’s humanity and the embodied divine mind

Teresa’s God is not an abstract absolute alone. Christ’s humanity is central. Teresa resists spiritual programs that would leave behind the humanity of Christ as one advances. Her contemplative mind remains Christological: the soul learns to be with Christ, to represent him inwardly, to accompany him, to speak with him, and to receive him as living presence.

This matters philosophically because Teresa does not make the highest mind a disembodied abstraction. The Incarnate Christ is the divine mind in human form, and the human soul is educated by relation to him. In Libro de la Vida, Teresa recommends being with Christ, looking at him as he looks at the soul, accompanying him, asking, humbling oneself, and remaining with him rather than exhausting the understanding in analysis.

Thus, the Teresian mind is not purified by escaping relational, historical, and embodied mediation. It is purified through rightly ordered relation to the incarnate Lord.

15. Angels and angelic minds

Teresa’s angelology is less systematically developed than her account of the human soul, but it belongs to her mental ontology. A Teresian dictionary entry by Tomás Álvarez notes that Teresa treats angels as celestial, wholly incorporeal spirits rooted in Scripture, liturgy, and popular devotion; she mentions Michael, cherubim, seraphim, guardian angels, and angelic participation in mystical visions.

Angels function in Teresa as spiritual intelligences who know, assist, guard, communicate, and participate in the court of God. In Camino, Teresa says that the angels know the condition of their King, and that the humble speech of a simple soul may please him more than elegant learned reasoning without humility. This is a small but revealing claim: angelic intelligence is presented as a knowing participation in the divine court, but it does not replace humility or human prayer.

Álvarez also notes that Teresa sees angels as pure spirits “burning in love,” defenders against evil, and sometimes as bearers of divine messages.

For a reconstructed philosophy of mind, angels represent finite spiritual minds: incorporeal, personal, intelligent, affectively ordered by love, and subordinate to God. They are not merely symbols of mental functions. They are part of the interpersonal spiritual environment in which the human soul lives.

16. Demonic minds and the limits of deception

Demons are also minds in Teresa’s world, but finite, fallen, deceptive, and limited. Teresa takes them seriously, yet she also sharply limits their power. This is an important balance.

In the fifth mansions, she says that in true union the devil cannot enter or do harm because God is so joined to the essence of the soul that the devil does not dare approach and does not understand the secret. She adds that demons are said not to understand our thought; still less can they understand a secret not entrusted even to thought.

This is a major claim about demonic cognition. Demons are not omniscient. They may disturb, tempt, imitate, suggest, and deceive, especially through imagination, memory, fear, false humility, and counterfeit communications. But they do not possess unrestricted access to the soul’s deepest center, nor can they reproduce the full effects of divine union.

Spanish scholarship has emphasized this distinctive Teresian weakening of the demonic enemy. Facundo Sebastián Macías argues that Teresa constructs, against the background of early modern demonological concern, a relatively “weak enemy” before contemplative prayer: still dangerous, but limited, especially where humility, prayer, and divine action are present.

This has philosophical consequences. Teresa’s world is not one in which deceptive intelligences make knowledge impossible. Demonic deception creates a need for discernment, humility, and testing; it does not collapse the possibility of truth. Unlike a radical skeptical demon, Teresa’s demons are finite agents whose effects can be identified by their fruits and whose access is restricted by God.

17. Mental pathology, melancholy, and pastoral realism

Teresa’s discernment is careful partly because she knows that not every troubling interior event is demonic and not every extraordinary event is divine. She repeatedly mentions melancholy, weak imagination, bodily causes, and natural disorder. In Las Moradas, she warns that a weak imagination or natural causes should not simply be blamed on the soul.

In her treatment of locutions, she says melancholy can fabricate experiences in the imagination and that spiritual directors should be careful rather than terrorizing or condemning such persons.

This is philosophically significant because Teresa’s mind is not a simplistic battlefield of God and devil. It includes natural causation, bodily temperament, weakness, illness, imagination, habit, sin, grace, and supernatural intervention. A good philosophy of mind must distinguish among them.

Her model also has therapeutic implications. She often recommends patience, humility, consultation, gentle redirection, non-identification with intrusive thoughts, and perseverance. In some cases, she advises not forcing prayer when the understanding is too disordered, but doing another work of virtue.

18. Agency, passivity, and consent

One of Teresa’s great insights is that passivity need not mean loss of personhood. In infused contemplation, the soul does not manufacture the experience. God acts; the soul receives. But reception is not nothing. The soul consents, surrenders, disposes itself, perseveres, remains humble, and later acts in charity.

In the third degree of prayer, Teresa says the soul sees clearly that it has done little or nothing except consent to the Lord’s gifts and embrace them with the will.

This yields a subtle theory of agency. Human agency has at least three modes:

Active effort: meditation, recollection, virtue, obedience, prayer, consultation.

Receptive consent: allowing God to act, not interfering, not forcing, not fleeing.

Transformed action: charity, service, courage, humility, reform, and love of neighbor after contemplation.

For Teresa, the highest passivity produces the strongest agency. The soul that has been most deeply recollected by God becomes more capable of service. In the seventh mansions, Teresa says the soul does not simply become absorbed and useless; it is more able to attend to God’s service than before.

This is a decisive anti-quietist point. The final mind is contemplative-active. Union does not end in private interior enjoyment but in works, love, suffering, service, and concern for the Church.

19. Ethical criterion: love of God and neighbor

Teresa’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from ethics. The truth of mental transformation is shown by the will’s conformity to God and by love of neighbor. In the fifth mansions, she says that true union can be found in doing God’s will and that God’s will is perfection; love of God and neighbor are the works on which one must labor.

This means the final criterion of a contemplative state is not phenomenological intensity. It is moral fruit. A rapture without humility is suspect. A vision without charity is spiritually dangerous. A locution that produces pride is not to be trusted. A quiet prayer that leads to greater love, courage, and service is more credible.

Teresa’s theory of mind is therefore virtue-epistemological. Mental states are assessed by their relation to virtue and by the kind of person they form. The mind is not healthy merely when it is clear, calm, or intense. It is healthy when it loves rightly.

20. Teresa and modern philosophy of mind

Teresa’s account can be placed in conversation with several modern themes, though it should not be reduced to them.

First, she anticipates a distinction between attention and involuntary thought. Modern cognitive science distinguishes executive attention from spontaneous thought, rumination, intrusive imagery, and mind-wandering. Teresa makes a spiritually precise version of this distinction when she separates thought/imagination from understanding and will.

Second, she offers a layered model of selfhood. The self is not identical with conscious contents. There is surface turbulence and deeper orientation; there are faculties and a center; there is soul and spirit.

Third, she gives an account of affective cognition. Love can disclose, order, and transform. The will is not blind appetite but a cognitive-affective power capable of knowing God by adherence.

Fourth, she offers a non-reductive embodied psychology. The body matters, but the soul is not reducible to bodily processes. Mental and spiritual events can have somatic signs; bodily conditions can disturb cognition; grace can overflow into bodily experience.

Fifth, she has a sophisticated epistemology of anomalous experience. Rather than dismiss visions and locutions or accept them uncritically, she classifies them, tests them, submits them to communal-theological judgment, and evaluates their fruits.

Sixth, she resists both rationalism and anti-intellectualism. She values learning and doctrine but denies that discursive reasoning is the highest form of spiritual knowledge.

Seventh, she is not simply a precursor of Descartes. There are intriguing historical and thematic comparisons, especially around interiority and deceptive demons, but Teresa’s method is different. Her project is not hyperbolic doubt leading to indubitable self-certainty; it is prayerful discernment leading to humility, union, and love.

21. A systematic Teresian philosophy of mind

A reconstructed Teresian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following propositions. 1. The human soul is a created spiritual subject with interior depth. It is not reducible to body, behavior, discursive thought, or present conscious contents. 2. The soul has faculties, but the soul is not identical with its faculties. Understanding, will, memory, imagination, and thought are powers of the soul, not the whole soul. 3. The mind is internally stratified. Surface distraction can coexist with deeper recollection; peripheral faculties can be at war while the center remains at peace. 4. Thought and imagination are not identical with understanding. Wandering thought is often involuntary and should not be automatically treated as culpable failure. 5. The will is the faculty of love, consent, and affective orientation. The highest transformation of mind occurs through conformity of the will to God. 6. Discursive understanding is good and necessary, especially in early prayer, but it is not the highest form of cognition. 7. Higher contemplation is receptive and infused. The soul cannot manufacture it by technique; God acts in the soul, sometimes suspending ordinary operations of the faculties. 8. There are non-discursive cognitive states. Intellectual visions and infused lights are not sensory images or ordinary inferences, yet Teresa treats them as knowledge-bearing. 9. The human mind is embodied. Bodily weakness, melancholy, senses, passions, tears, breath, and posture are part of the actual economy of prayer and consciousness. 10. Mental events require discernment. Their source may be God, angelic mediation, demonic deception, imagination, melancholy, natural weakness, or self-love. 11. God is the supreme mind and the indwelling center of the soul. Divine cognition is not passive observation but transformative communication. 12. Angels and demons are finite spiritual minds. Angels assist and communicate under God; demons deceive and disturb but are limited, especially before true union and humility. 13. The test of contemplative cognition is ethical fruit: humility, peace, love, obedience, courage, and service. 14. The telos of mind is not self-enclosed certainty but loving union: the soul becomes most itself by being joined to God and poured out in love of neighbor.

22. The deepest originality: a non-egological interiority

The most distinctive feature of Teresa’s philosophy of mind is that it is interior without being ego-centered. Modern interiority often moves toward self-possession: I know myself, I analyze myself, I secure myself, I authenticate myself. Teresa’s interiority moves toward dispossession: I enter myself and find that I am not my own center. God is there before me.

The castle is “mine,” but its deepest room belongs to God. The faculties are “mine,” but they can be gathered, suspended, illuminated, or transformed by another. The will is “mine,” but its perfection is to be conformed to God’s will. The mind is not fulfilled by autonomous self-presence; it is fulfilled by receptive communion.

This is why Teresa’s treatment of God, angels, demons, and supernatural communications is not a theological distraction. It is the cosmology within which her philosophy of mind operates. The human mind is one kind of mind within a larger order of minds: divine, angelic, demonic, ecclesial, and human. But these minds are not equal. God is the source and end; angels are finite spiritual helpers; demons are finite deceivers; human minds are embodied, wounded, educable, and capable of union.

23. Final synthesis

Teresa’s philosophy of mind is best described as a contemplative faculty-personalism. It is a faculty psychology because she analyzes understanding, will, memory, imagination, thought, senses, and passions. It is personalist because the soul is more than these faculties and is ordered toward interpersonal communion. It is contemplative because the highest cognition is receptive, loving, and God-given. It is embodied because bodily and affective conditions are never irrelevant. It is theological because God, angels, demons, grace, Christ, and the Trinity are not external topics but constitutive features of the mental world she describes.

Her final answer to “what is the mind?” would not be “the thinking thing.” It would be something closer to this:

The mind is the living interior of a created soul, wounded and distractible, furnished with powers that can war against one another, capable of recollection through prayer, capable of love beyond discursive understanding, capable of being taught by God in its center, vulnerable to deception yet protected by humility and discernment, embodied in weakness and delight, and destined not for mere self-knowledge but for union with the God who already dwells within it.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
